comments.title: Thorough DMH Safety Question
comments.content:
1) This might be an excessively long question, if so, I quite understand. It is also, unfortunately, yet another magical-safety-around-kids question. In case the answer is very simple, to save you time, here it is baldly: are any of the rituals given in the DMH unsafe around young children/pregnant women?

Based on my current knowledge and your previous answers, I suspect the answer might be "yes, some of them are unsafe", so in the hopes of making this question a learning experience for myself and other readers, rather than just a request for information, I've categorized the rituals by what seem the relevant differences in determining safety and listed each one with my best guess for the answer to "Is it safe to perform in the same house as small children/pregnant women, but in areas they don't frequent?"

A. Slight Variations on the Normal SOP - Safe
1. Explanation: you've said many times that the SOP is fine, so long as the kid doesn't spend much time in the place where you usually do it.
2. Normal, Daily SOP: Safe
3. SOP to transform yourself or the world around you: Safe
4. Opening a Grove with the SOP, but no other ritual work: Safe
5. Invoking a single Ogham Few with the SOP: Safe
B. Daily Grail Exercise in Various Stages of Completeness - Safe
1. Explanation: You've also mentioned the Grail Exercise as about as safe as the SOP, at least in it's daily-practice form
2. Imagining the Three Cauldrons: Safe
3. The Three Cauldrons Exercise: Safe
4. The Rising Dragons Exercise: Safe
5. The Tree of Light Exercise: Safe
C. Using the Heather and Oak Pentagrams and/or making contact with large flows of nwyfre for the first time - Unsafe?
1. Explanation: This category is the one I'm most confused on, and it has the most impact on my assumptions elsewhere. If calling up the Telluric and Solar currents in a more intensive way is unsafe, then these are most of the rituals where that seems to apply.
2. Awakening the Two Currents: Unsafe?
3. Invoking an Ogham Few within a Grove, with tools, and with the two pentagrams: Unsafe?
4. The Three Cauldrons Ritual: Unsafe?
5. The Rising Dragons Ritual: Unsafe?
6. The Tree of Light Ritual: Unsafe?
7. Cleansing the Land: Unsafe?
8. Standing Stone Working: Unsafe?
D. Enchanting the Druid Tools - Mixed
1. Explanation: Each of these seems to broadly follow the logic of one of the categories above, and so I think will go as it went
2. Enchanting a Circle of Stones: Safe. See A.
3. Enchanting a Wand: Unsafe. See C.
4. Enchanting a Cauldron: Unsafe. See C., but possibly even more so, since you repeat for each station of the year
5. Enchanting the Crane Bag: Safe? This one is a bit different from the others, but since the cauldron (rather than the currents directly) is used as the source of the nwyfre, this seems more "contained"
6. Enchanting other objects (talismans, etc): Would follow the logic of whichever ritual method you based it on
E. Other - Depends
1. Consecrating Sacred Spaces: Depends. Will depend on what ritual models are used

replies.title: Re: Thorough DMH Safety Question
replies.content:
You know, it fascinates me that no matter how simple and straightforward I try to make this, people insist on overthinking it and twisting everything I've said out of shape. Please read the following paragraph from the Magic Monday FAQ over and over again until you realize that it answers all your questions:

"If your children are under the age of three, basic magical practices such as the Sphere of Protection, the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, or the Middle Pillar exercise are fine, but don’t do them in any place where your children spend much of their time. Any more complex magical working? Leave it until later."

In particular, please read the phrase "any more complex magical working" and the words that follow it. Okay, now read it again. And again. And again. Now read the phrase "basic magical practices" and what follows it. Try to grasp the meaning of the phrase "basic magical practices." Notice that this does in fact mean "basic magical practices," and not anything other than "basic magical practices." Now notice that things other than basic magical practices are not, in fact, basic magical practices. (I really am hoping that if I repeat this often enough it will finally sink in.)

One more time: the ordinary, basic, unelaborated Sphere of Protection counts as a basic magical practice. The ordinary, basic, daily Grail Working also counts. None of the other things you've listed qualify. They aren't basic magical practices. No, they're not basic magical practices, and in fact, they're not basic magical practices. Nor are they basic magical practices.

Now for the love of the gods, WILL YOU PLEASE JUST STOP?

replies.title: Re: Thorough DMH Safety Question
replies.content:
I have given the "Why are magical practices unsafe for young children?" question some thought. I believe ritual magic practice around young children is unsafe and potentially harmful to them for these reasons:

1. Ritual magic grabs the attention of various entities who otherwise would just pass by. Though ritual magic invokes divine forces, it looks like a fireworks show on the astral plane. Or at least that's what a friend who died told me shortly after his death. Even an innocuous ritual such as the Sphere of Protection is quite the attention getter. Now this would not be an issue, except...

2. Little kids have a deep astral and etheric connection to their parents, whether or not they are biologically related. The connection is so deep in the beginning it's almost like sharing a body. Infants especially are nearly symbiotic with mom and dad. Overall, babies are not just absorbent sponges in the learning sense. They are absorbent in every sense. That's why we don't want to grab the attention of incorporeal beings who could potentially (and perhaps unintentionally) harm them.

3. Ritual magic, including the Sphere of Protection and the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram, deals in polarities. This is expressed through the male and female currents invoked in order to create a magical "charge". Exposing children to this charge will fry their circuitry, maybe not physically, but in every other aspect. Natural magic doesn't have this problem because it's gentler and you're working with the etheric plane. Etheric plane magic doesn't pose danger unless you are doing something dumb like trying to hex someone with a poppet.

Not that this will make any parent stop, but it is my opinion that any parent who does ritual magic around their young child is putting their own curiosity above their child's well-being and safety, and not just a little bit. In my opinion, doing ritual magic around little kids poses similar dangers to leaving a baby alone and naked in a room frequented by perverts.

I was able to deduce the above reasons with an extremely modest amount of discursive meditation.

JMG,

I'm getting into my 3rd year of magical practice in the GD tradition, and I would say on most days of the last 3 years I've performed the LBRP, R+C, MP, divination and discursive meditation. I've been experiencing a curious state of mind, and I was wondering whether anyone has given it a name before, since I haven't seen it mentioned anywhere. Basically, it feels as though I'm permanently in a slightly altered state of consciousness, as if my mind is not fully in my body, but slightly outside, or above it (spatial metaphors fall short here). This isn't a full-on OBE, but more of a sense of detachment or dissociation from the concerns of the body, including sensations, emotions and passions. If the experience of the body is like a storm, this is like being in the eye of the storm. This state seems to be associated with more acute self-awareness and being able to observe internal phenomena from a third person perspective, as they were happening to a stranger rather than to oneself. At the same time, there's a feeling almost of energy and invigoration, something like the buzz one gets from caffeine, but more sustained and without the apparent downsides. I would compare it to being in the zone while reading a good book or the ecstatic feeling of listening to the climax of a good piece of music. I would appreciate hearing your thoughts on this. And by the way, I believe that I have an adequate amount of red meat in my diet and get enough exercise.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's called "detachment," and it's one of the gifts of magical practice: you become aware that you are not your body, and that the body and its concerns are not as central to you as you once thought. You're in a less altered state of consciousness than most people -- the standard human state of consciousness has been compared to dozing or drunkenness, and you've begun to wake up and sober up. Welcome to a more real world!

Or wait out the blank phase until more ideas come? Recycle the ideas and consider them again?  My question is when to stop?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In the early phases, set yourself a time -- say, ten minutes of discursive meditation, starting when you've finished all the preliminaries. Have a clock in a place where you can see it without moving your head. If you run out of things, pause to see if anything else surfaces after a moment, and if not, go back through the sequence of thoughts you've followed out and see if any new angles come to mind. Keep going until you've done your ten minutes, and then stop. Later on, when you're more experienced, you won't have the blank spaces and you can be a little more flexible on time.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content: Nah, the meat is there because we need it in order to learn, grow, and ripen spiritually. So are the crappy jobs and psychotic bosses. To complete our journey through the the realm of incarnate life, as the Triads say, we have to be all things, know all things, and suffer all things: it's all part of the alchemy by which we refine ourselves for higher and more interesting modes of existence. As for the relative merits of life and afterlife, why, the conditions of your next afterlife are determined by how you spend this life, of course. It can be heaven or hell, depending on how you handle things here and now. To the best of my recollection, I've had good and bad lives, and good and bad afterlives, and in each case I got what I earned.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content: By no coincidence at all, I was planning to ask you a related question. You've spoken about afterlives a number of times, but it wasn't clear to me whether there are reputable accounts by people who have memories of them. You obviously do. Is there much of a literature on this as seen from a magical perspective?

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content: There's some. Dion Fortune's book Through the Gates of Death covers the firt part of the afterlife in quite some detail; the chapter The Soul in Judgment in Yeats' A Vision is a good summary of occult teaching, and several books by Max Heindel, George Winslow Plummer, and other Rosicrucian writers have quite a bit to say about the same subject. replies.title: Re: Questions of Practice
replies.content:
If I might offer a few resources on the Runes:

A) This list of "The 10 Best Books on the Runes" is pretty thorough, and includes explanations of what is valuable about each, from the purely academic to the rather esoteric: https://norse-mythology.org/runes/the-best-books-on-the-runes/

I do not recommend feeling like you need to read all of these before you begin learning about the Runes. If you're looking for something to quickly get you started on practical divination, I think either The Runecaster's Handbook by Thorsson or Nordic Runes by Paul Rhys Mountfort are the way to go. If you want to start with a minimum of other people's thoughts and start with the very basic meanings of the Rune names, then The Rune Primer by Plowright or Rudiments of Runelore by Pollington are good choices.

B) Whatever source you use for divination, I also strongly recommend spending some time meditating on each Rune - I had done a lot of reading before I started meditating on them, and the meditation led to a huge jump in my understanding. The Nine Doors of Midgard, which is the Rune Gild's magical training program, recommends spending 9 days on each rune, which is very thorough, but potentially helpful. As for what themes to use, here are a few options:

i. Just take the Rune itself (its name, shape, sound, meaning of the name) as your theme and go from there. Even if you make use of the other theme suggestions, this can be helpful for the surpsises you might find

ii. Use the stanzas of one or more of the Rune Poems as your themes. The Anglo-Saxon Rune poem is good, especially if you're working with the Elder Futhark, since it has a stanza for every Rune, but the Norwegian and Icelandic poems give some further perspective. You can find a translation of the Rune Poems in Thorsson's Runelore, as well as in a just-published collection The Rune Poems, edited by P.D. Brown and Michael Moynihan, which also includes several modern poems about the Runes, written by poets with esoteric practices involving the runes

iii. The Teutonic Way: Magic by Kveldulf Gunnarsson has a set of guided visualizations for the Runes (like scrying, but with a script). I found these far more helpful than I expected and arrived at some insights I wouldn't have otherwise, but I also didn't try them until after I had done lots of other meditating on the Runes, and different folks respond differently to such guided meditations

C) A minor practical tip: for daily readings, I like using a leather dice rolling tray. I shake the runes, drop them into the tray, and then without looking, draw three for Urdhr, Verdhandi, Skuld. This allows me to literally "cast" the runes without them flying all over the place. Also, decide ahead of time whether you're going to use reversed/mirk readings, and if so, decide on a consistent way to count a stave as reversed (the two options are to do it by orientation when turned face up, like with a tarot card, or by face up/face down)

Hope some of this helps, and good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Another round of Internet Magic ...
replies.content:
The design of Carroll's sigil strikes me as utterly wretched:

(1) The center symbol is made from the five Latin-alphabet letters used to spell Putin's surname in the West. And the cross that appears to occupy its center looks like a Latin, Western cross--though it is most likely just an accident arising from the Latin-alphabet letters T and I. But Putin is (obviously) a Russian, and writes his name in the Cyrillic alphabet: П У Т И Н.

Would a magician doing a magicial working against China's head-of-state reasonaby expect to get results using a Romanization of the Chinese characters that make up that person's name? There's nothing special about the Latin alphabet that makes it universally applicable in magic.

(2) Putin is also a baptized Orrthodox Christian, and the cross that he probably wears underneath his shirt almost constantly wil be the Russian Orthodox cross with three crossbars, the upper and lower ones shorter than the middle one, and the lower crossbar slightly tilted. This cross, which most baptized Russians wear pretty much 24/7, also gives spiritual protection against any human or demonic attack. (Russians mostly take demons quite seriously, but fear them much less than Westerners do, though they usually won't admit it to Westerners.)

(3) The man himself is actually just Vladimir Vladimirovich on a very basic level, and in almost all face-to-face situations another Russian would call him that. Family names serve to distinguish between Russians with the same baptismal name (Vladimir) and patronymic (Vladimirovich = son of Vladimir). There are, or have been, many cultures where the very idea of a family name makes no sense whatever, and peoples' names are simply their given name plus their patronymic. Russia had one of those cultures until only a few centuries ago, and strong traces of that past are still there just beneath the surface in its present-day culture.

Basically, magic worked for or against a person has to be worked in terms of the targeted person's culture, not the magician's culture. Magic done by Westerners won't have much of an impact on non-Westerners unless the magician is knowledgeable enough to work with the symbols and norms of their non-Western cultures, not his own Western culture.

replies.title: Re: Another round of Internet Magic ...
replies.content: Those are excellent points. One of the classic modes of failure of Chaos magic -- and of course it's shared by a lot of pop-culture magic -- is that too many practitioners never quite seem to realize that anything matters outside the boundaries of their own egos. "I understand what the symbol means, and that's all that matters" -- I've fielded that insistence far too often, sometimes from people who ought to know better. I expect any day to hear that they picked up the phone, dialed a random number, said "Wthotyluinauntei vmiunlkbhfw nvnfisklhv" into it, and then got all irate because the pizza they meant to order never showed up!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for asking that question.

For what it's worth, I once had a similar type of experience.

When I was in kindergarten, I became friendly with a girl, and we grew up together through elementary and junior high school. We spent a lot of time together in the early grades, then drifted apart a bit in junior high, but remained on friendly terms until graduating from 8th grade. We then went to different high schools, and I lost contact with her.

I clearly remember the spring day near the end of college when I was sitting in a classroom. It was a beautiful day, and I was only half paying attention to what was going on in class, distracted by the open window and warm breeze. All of a sudden, I was hit with a wave of grief. It just came out of nowhere, this feeling of loss and sadness - but I didn't know for whom, as I hadn't experienced a recent loss. Logically, it could have been chalked up to sadness at the end of my college days and saying goodbye to friends, but it didn't feel like that - I was mostly looking forward to finishing college, and that day I hadn't been moping about the end of school, I'd been wool-gathering about mundane things (probably lunch). The exact way I described it in my mind at the time was that it felt like someone I knew had died, but I had no idea who.

I was actually kind of freaked out, and when I got back to my dorm I checked in with friends (they were all fine) and called home that night, "just to say hi", but really to check on them, too. Nothing. Everyone was fine. I eventually wrote it off as inexplicable, and all but forgot about it.

I'm sure you see where this is going.

Years later, I came across the obituary of that childhood friend, and discovered she'd died the summer before our senior year in college. And I was immediately certain it had been her. As soon as I saw her date of death, I remembered how that wave of grief that hit me in a college classroom had felt like the loss of someone from long ago - it had felt like HER.

I don't know how or why, but I feel like she "stopped by" some months after her death for a fleeting goodbye. We hadn't been in touch in almost 8 years, but we'd been friends as kids - and it was like she just somehow wanted to say farewell to her childhood friend.

Maybe these things happen from time to time.

comments.title: Mercury retrograde and new software releases
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've been asked to be on the release team for a software project that has a yearly release schedule. Looking at past releases and this year's calendar, there is a good chance that this year's release would fall during a Mercury retrograde. Insofar as I am able to justify in terms that normies can accept (i.e. beta testing reports), would it be worth it to try to avoid the retrograde?

replies.title: Re: Mercury retrograde and new software releases
replies.content: Yes. If you release it during the retrograde, odds are there will be a bunch of problems that won't be caught in time, and you'll have to scramble to fix them once Mercury turns around. Wait until Mercury's moving forward again, and you'll tend to have much less trouble.

replies.title: Re: Mercury retrograde and new software releases
replies.content:
I will go ahead and state my professional opinion that "there will be a bunch of problems that won't be caught in time, and you'll have to scramble to fix them" is the baseline for the whole industry. If you add Mercury's retrograde on top of that, chances are the product will be pretty much unusable at release date.
If I had the gumption to put myself in that position again, I'd burn as much social capital as safely possible to make sure version control and testing standards are followed *to* *the* *letter* ("trust but verify"; you'd be surprised to the lengths some nerds will go to avoid enduring a stern glare during weekly meetings). That way, you will have hard data to prove if and when release-date should be pushed forward into the near future. If it gets bad enough, you may kick the can all the way to the other side.

comments.title: blocking unwanted psychic influence from communications media
comments.content:
Dear JMG and commentariat,

As ivn666 wrote in MM on 14 February, TV indoctrinates one with "the imagery and suggested emotional framework" of others' agendas. Likewise commercial radio and the programming of shop music.

Anonymous the same day writing about their repressed "Onion Head" exposure and how wrong it felt is a case of similar impact.

JMG, you called this kind of thing "cheap sorcery" and I am one of those acutely sensitive to it. On a certain level I can't bear the imagery of animated characters either. A poster for something like a Pixar film repels me most strongly. If unlucky enough to walk into a room with the film showing, I'm likely to be grabbed by other aspects of the production (the emotional hooks, music, technical expertise) and afterwards feel quite dirty, as though I'd been half-willingly compromised.

I may avoid these things at home. In the outside world it is another matter. Whenever I catch a bus, go to grocery stores or attend a waiting room they swamp me. And I find it unsettling, nigh unbearable.

It could be akin to what syfen described in the same MM as his "nagging sense of discomfort bordering on anxiety" accompanying increased awareness from Will training. I could flatter myself that I've been developing willpower and attaining spiritual awareness without effort... Trouble is that this discomfort has been happening for a very long time.

The radio part is definitely associated with social class preferences - my horror of the coarse supposedly witty discourse of the announcers for instance - but these programming tricks have been creeping steadily into public broadcasting as well, which had been previously more highbrow and to me seemed removed from the manipulation.

So, to paraphrase ivn666 once more, Is there anything I can do other than my routine banishings and reading to help keep this stuff out of my aura?

Many thanks

comments.title: Tattoos
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you for hosting this space again.

A friend of mine has a tattoo, of his own design, incorporating many different esoteric symbols. He got it during a very unstable phase of heavy drug use and Crowleyite dabblings, which he now calls his "Icarus phase". The tattoo signified the pledging of his soul to Lucifer, whom he regarded at the time as the true God, and of whom he believed himself to be the living incarnation.

What are some 'damage control' methods for this man? He now has a strong relationship with Christ, to whom he has since pledged himself in service to, and resonates with the teachings of Steiner. He is also very closely involved with a spiritual teacher called Dave Oshana, who appears to be pretty wholesome (no cult-like dynamics, focused on energy work and body awareness).

On a related note, I have an eight-pointed chaos star and an Eye of Horus from my own phase of drug-dabbling. The Eye is, unfortunately, from a design used in one of Crowley's books, but I believe he lifted that from the Golden Dawn, so I'm not too worried about that one. The chaos star, however, does perturb me somewhat, given what I have seen coming out of the chaos-magic scene. Do you think there could be any harmful effects from these tattoos, and if so, what are some ways to counteract them?

Best wishes,
Luke

replies.title: Re: Tattoos
replies.content:
You should both be fine. The important thing is to fill the gap with something more positive; once you've done that, except in very specific circumstances (and if you chose the tattoo yourselves, those don't apply), an occult-themed tattoo is no more of an issue than one of those romantic tattoos that you get about a week before you break up with the other person, and regard ruefully ever after.

The Eye of Horus, by the way, is a potent symbol of spiritual protection; ancient Egyptians wore it the way Christians in the sacramental churches wear cross or crucifix pendants, for cimilar reasons and with similar effects.

replies.title: Re: Tattoos
replies.content: Many thanks!

Dear JMG,

1. I did a Geomancy reading on the question, "Is Hu Gadarn the same God as Lugus?" The answer I got:

LW: Puella
RW: Puer
Judge: Conjunctio

To me this seems like a "kinda yes" answer. Conjunctio is associated with Mercury and I think the Romans associated Lugus with Mercury (though they might have done the same with one or more other Celtic deities). These two witnesses seem to indicate a balance of polarities or at least genders, which seems to be rather Mercurial as well. Am I totally off-base here or do you think according to this there might be a firm association of Hu with Mercury? Or might this simply point to Hu representing a balance of forces?

2. Have you ever read/heard about Roman Mithraism having a strong influence on the medieval Welsh Bardic tradition? I read somewhere recently (I forget where exactly) that Taliesin the Bard may have been a low-key Mithraist and that he encoded some of its symbolism in his works. Iolo seems to have picked up on this many centuries later and in Barrdas he makes an obscure association with Mithras being his own version of "God the Son" following Celi as being "God the Father." And in the next section he associates Hu Gararn with Jesus. Following on the above inquiry, might then Hu be a reflex of Mithras or a deity represented in the syncretistic Roman Mithraic pantheon?

~Obsidian Impertinent Sloth

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's about as perfect a definite maybe as I've ever seen geomancy produce. Conjunctio is "maybe yes, maybe no," and you've got one favorable figure and one unfavorable figure as its parents.

2) A strong influence? No. An influence? Possibly. There was a Mithraeum at the Roman fort in Segontium (today's Caernarfon) -- you can see its remains if you go there -- and there's reason to think that at least some of the old Mysteries survived in Britain through the dark ages -- Jessie Weston's books on the Grail legends are worth reading in that context. Hu is a weird, syncretic figure more or less invented by Iolo Morganwg out of a mix of medieval legend and archaic myth, so it's possible that some Mithraic raw material may have found its way into the bubbling pot.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

Could you give a synopsis of the supernal triangle of Kether, Chokmah, and Binah; the Abyss; and Daath? Specifically, how they relate to one another and how we can (or cannot) interact with them.

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. The Supernals, the three highest spheres of the Tree, are the principles from which created existence comes into being. Binah is pure abstract form, Chokmah is pure abstract force, and Kether is pure existence not otherwise specified in any way. All three of them are beyond our comprehension, and their interactions are equally unknowable.

For that matter, we can't interact with them directly at all: the Abyss, the gap between the supernal triad and the lower seven spheres, is the barrier between transcendent reality and the limited, created reality we inhabit, and that barrier is absolute. What we can interact with is the reflection of the supernal triad through the Abyss; that reflection is Daath. It's like the spring where underground waters come surging up onto the surface. We can't interact with the underground source of the waters, but once the water comes to the surface we can interact with it.

comments.title: Orpheus and the Roots of Platonism

It seems I was reading a comment here recently asking about sources on Orpheus and Orphism...but I can't seem to find the comment.

In the chance that that commenter will see this comment, I came across this title at work today, which may be useful:

Orpheus and the Roots of Platonism by Algis Uzdavinys, published by Matheson Trust for Comparative Religion in 2011.

"A book on the religious, mystic origins and substance of philosophy. This is a critical survey of ancient and modern sources and of scholarly works dealing with Orpheus and everything related to this major figure of ancient Greek myth, religion and philosophy. Here poetic madness meets religious initiation and Platonic philosophy. This book contains fascinating insights into the usually downplaid relations between Egyptian initiation, Greek mysteries and Plato's philosophy and followers, right into Hellenistic Neoplatonic and Hermetic developments."

(I saw it listed in the back of the enticing "Sacred royalty: from the Pharaoh to the Most Christian King" by Jean Hani.) The Matheson Monographs series has some rather interesting titles!

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: The Vault of the Adepts in GD Work

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Of course you can. You might consider doing some of the Golden Section work in the evening, for example.

2) You can always pick a new theme, or learn to discipline your mind so you don't run through all the themes the night before!

3) I'm quite certain that they did.

4) Yes, you can use a different grove ritual. You might want to check out my recent book The Druid Path, which gives a closely related grove ritual that has no sword in it.

5) Every energy center in the body -- there are 360 of them -- has different effects on the subtle bodies. The heart center is worked with later in the DMH system, but its energies are mingled with those of the womb center in the abdomen, so a point midway between them is useful.

6) In the WGS, the version of the SoP used is designed to be used with a range of different inner practices, and thus it doesn't specify either the heart or the solar plexus center. The solar plexus exercise in that system is much more general, and helps clear blockages from the whole middle part of the body. replies.content: There are various ways to do it. Make a list of them, meditate on them, decide which one works best for you, and go with that. I mean this quite seriously. replies.title: Re: Mirrored Geomantic chart
replies.content:
Always start with the witnesses and the judge. Something is ending, and something else is going to begin, but right now you're between those and nothing much is happening.

From there -- well, have you gotten to the four triplicities yet? Each figure in each of the triplicities is assigned a specific meaning in terms of your life, and you should be able to read those out easily enough by taking the figure as an indication for what will happen in that part of your life. Don't worry about the figures mirroring -- simply pay attention to what each figure says in its place.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thanks as always for taking time to answer questions.

1. When approaching diet and magic, have you found that ingesting animal protein has had any effects on your practice? I understand that most of the literature is based on mystics, who tend towards vegetarianism, which skews things.

2. Is there any sort of smell test advice you'd give new practitioners when figuring out if a teacher really knows their stuff?

3. Manley Hall was seekingly able to comingle Shingon Buddhism with western esotericism and you've made comments indicating Japanese practices seem to mesh well with western occulticism too. Is it because each tradition has similar goals or their practices tend to affect the subtle bodies in similar fashions?

Thanks,

Tamanous

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) A vegetarian diet is a bad idea for anyone who practices ceremonial magic. Meat helps you ground yourself in your body, which after all is made of meat, and you need that to balance out the intense energies that ceremonial magic generates. It so happens that, like a lot of people of Celtic ancestry, I don't assimilate vegetable proteins well -- I've done vegetarian diets at some points in my life, early on, and every time I ended up constantly chilled and catching every cold that came through. A bacon cheeseburger fixed it every time.

2) Common sense is a very good guide here. If a prospective teacher's personal life is a mess, walk away. If their students don't make you want to be like them, the same rule applies. A good teacher can manage his financial and emotional affairs without exploiting his students, and his senior students have benefited from the teaching and have become thoughtful, functional, interesting people.

3) It's more complex than that. There are some very odd similarities between European Pagan and Japanese tradition, the sort of thing that make one wonder if both ends of Eurasia held onto traditions that were lost elsewhere. Whether or not that's the cause of it, I've found that many elements of Japanese occultism seem to fit very well with Western occultism, and this extends into occult-influenced practices such as Do-in and aikido.
